def solve(grid)
  m = grid.length
  return 0 if m == 0
  n = grid[0].length
  return 0 if n == 0

  MAX_XOR_VALUE = 1023
  
  # m_dp[i][j] stores a bitmask where the k-th bit is set 
  # if XOR sum k is achievable at (i, j).
  m_dp = Array.new(m) { Array.new(n, 0) }

  # Base case: (0, 0)
  initial_value = grid[0][0]
  m_dp[0][0] = (1 << initial_value)

  # Iterate through all cells
  for i in 0...m
    for j in 0...n
      next if i == 0 && j == 0
      
      current_value = grid[i][j]
      current_mask = 0
      
      # 1. Contribution from Top (i-1, j)
      if i > 0
        prev_mask = m_dp[i - 1][j]
        
        # Iterate through all possible previous XOR sums k
        (0..MAX_XOR_VALUE).each do |k|
          # Check if XOR sum k was possible at (i-1, j)
          if (prev_mask & (1 << k)) != 0
            # If k was possible, then k XOR current_value is possible now
            new_xor = k ^ current_value
            current_mask |= (1 << new_xor)
          end
        end
      end

      # 2. Contribution from Left (i, j-1)
      if j > 0
        prev_mask = m_dp[i][j - 1]
        
        # Iterate through all possible previous XOR sums k
        (0..MAX_XOR_VALUE).each do |k|
          # Check if XOR sum k was possible at (i, j-1)
          if (prev_mask & (1 << k)) != 0
            # If k was possible, then k XOR current_value is possible now
            new_xor = k ^ current_value
            current_mask |= (1 << new_xor)
          end
        end
      end
      
      m_dp[i][j] = current_mask
    end
  end

  final_mask = m_dp[m - 1][n - 1]
  
  min_xor = MAX_XOR_VALUE + 1 

  # Find the smallest k (minimum XOR value) whose bit is set in the final mask
  (0..MAX_XOR_VALUE).each do |k|
    if (final_mask & (1 << k)) != 0
      min_xor = k
      break 
    end
  end

  return min_xor
end

$M$ dimensions: $m \times n$.

Initialization:
$M$ is initialized to 0.
$M[0][0] = (1 \ll \text{grid}[0][0])$.

Iteration:
Iterate $i$ from $0$ to $m-1$.
Iterate $j$ from $0$ to $n-1$.
If $(i, j) = (0, 0)$, continue (already initialized).

For $(i, j)$:
$v = \text{grid}[i][j]$.
$CurrentMask = 0$.

// Contribution from above (i-1, j)
if $i > 0$:
  $PrevMask = M[i-1][j]$.
  // If $k$ was reachable at $(i-1, j)$, then $k \oplus v$ is reachable at $(i, j)$.
  // We need to calculate the mask for $\{ k \oplus v \mid k \in \text{set represented by } PrevMask \}$.
  // This is a XOR transformation on the set.
  // If we iterate over all $k$ (0 to 1023):
  for $k=0$ to 1023:
    if $(PrevMask \gg k) \& 1$:
      $CurrentMask = CurrentMask | (1 \ll (k \oplus v))$.

// Contribution from left (i, j-1)
if $j > 0$:
  $PrevMask = M[i][j-1]$.
  for $k=0$ to 1023:
    if $(PrevMask \gg k) \& 1$:
      $CurrentMask = CurrentMask | (1 \ll (k \oplus v))$.

$M[i][j] = CurrentMask$.

Wait, we are calculating $M[i][j]$ based on $M[i-1][j]$ and $M[i][j-1]$. If we process row by row, column by column, $M[i-1][j]$ and $M[i][j-1]$ will be computed before $M[i][j]$. This looks correct.

Let's refine the loop structure. We must iterate through all $(i, j)$ in order.

$m = \text{grid.length}$
$n = \text{grid}[0].length$
Max value $V_{max} = 1023$.

$M = \text{Array}(m).fill(0).map(() => \text{Array}(n).fill(0))$

$M[0][0] = 1 \ll \text{grid}[0][0]$

For $i = 0$ to $m-1$:
  For $j = 0$ to $n-1$:
    if $i == 0$ and $j == 0$: continue

    $v = \text{grid}[i][j]$
    $CurrentMask = 0$

    // 1. From Top
    if $i > 0$:
      $PrevMask = M[i-1][j]$
      for $k=0$ to $V_{max}$:
        if $(PrevMask \& (1 \ll k))$: // Check if XOR sum k was possible
          $CurrentMask = CurrentMask | (1 \ll (k \oplus v))$

    // 2. From Left
    if $j > 0$:
      $PrevMask = M[i][j-1]$
      for $k=0$ to $V_{max}$:
        if $(PrevMask \& (1 \ll k))$: // Check if XOR sum k was possible
          $CurrentMask = CurrentMask | (1 \ll (k \oplus v))$

    $M[i][j] = CurrentMask$

Final result:
The minimum XOR value is the smallest $k$ such that the $k$-th bit of $M[m-1][n-1]$ is set.

We iterate $k$ from 0 to 1023 and return the first $k$ where $M[m-1][n-1] \& (1 \ll k)$ is non-zero.

Let's check edge cases:
1. $1 \times 1$ grid: $m=1, n=1$. $M[0][0] = 1 \ll \text{grid}[0][0]$. We skip the loops. Result is $\text{grid}[0][0]$. The code handles this: $M[0][0]$ is set. We check $M[0][0]$ for the minimum set bit. This works.
2. $1 \times N$ grid (Row only): $i=0$ always. Only movement from left is possible. DP correctly calculates prefix XOR sums along the row.
3. $M \times 1$ grid (Column only): $j=0$ always. Only movement from top is possible. DP correctly calculates prefix XOR sums along the column.

The constraints state $m \times n \le 1000$. The complexity $O(MN \cdot 2^B)$ is fine. Since $M \times N \le 1000$ and $2^B = 1024$, the total operations are around $1000 \cdot 1024 \approx 10^6$ operations, which is very fast.

Implementation details in JavaScript:

We need to handle array initialization and bitwise operations correctly in JS. JS numbers are 64-bit floats, but bitwise operations treat them as 32-bit signed integers. Since $1023 < 2^{10}$, we are safe using standard integer arithmetic for bitmasks up to $2^{31}-1$.
